topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The misplaced laptop was the first clue. It lay open on the kitchen counter, the screen displaying a half-finished manuscript. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet apartment. I approached slowly, my feet feeling weighted, as if moving through thick water. The words swam before my eyes—descriptions of my morning routine, the way I habitually tucked my hair behind my ear, the specific, slightly anxious way I hummed while making coffee. A wave of unexpected heat bloomed in my chest, quickly followed by a chilling sense of exposure. I didn't want to touch it, didn't want to breathe the same air as the words, but I couldn't look away. When he walked in, whistling off-key, I flinched, my shoulders hunching inward. "Hey," he said, oblivious. I just nodded, my gaze fixed on the screen, a strange, tight knot forming just beneath my ribs.

[story 2]

I found the draft tucked inside a book he’d left on the bedside table. The ink was dark, the handwriting elegant, and the subject was undeniably me. My hands trembled as I traced a sentence—a line about my tendency to overthink every social interaction. A profound stillness settled over me, a heavy cloak dropping onto my skin. My palms began to sweat, cold dampness spreading up my arms. I backed away from the table, needing distance, needing to scrub the image from my mind. When he turned around, his expression shifting from casual to concerned, I felt a desperate urge to disappear. I mumbled something incoherent, the sound thin and reedy, focusing intently on the pattern of the rug beneath my bare feet, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying feeling of being utterly seen.

[story 3]

It wasn't until I saw the title—a deliberately evocative, slightly dramatic heading—that the reality of the situation crashed over me. My vision narrowed, the edges of the room blurring into an indistinct wash of color. A strange, hollow ache started in my throat, making swallowing difficult. I stood frozen by the doorway, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs. Every muscle in my body seemed to tighten simultaneously, a physical reaction to the weight of the unspoken. When he finally noticed my stillness, he paused, his brow furrowing with confusion. I managed a weak, shaky smile, a performance I didn't recognize, and quickly turned toward the hallway, needing to escape the suffocating atmosphere of the room.
